elements.sent-2411-001: It is widely acknowledged that the performance of Transformer models is exponentially related to their number of parameters and computational complexity.
elements.sent-2411-002: While approaches like Mixture of Experts (MoE) decouple parameter count from computational complexity, they still face challenges in inference due to high memory access costs.
elements.sent-2411-003: This work introduces UltraMem, incorporating large-scale, ultra-sparse memory layer to address these limitations.
elements.sent-2411-004: Our approach significantly reduces inference latency while maintaining model performance.
elements.sent-2411-005: We also investigate the scaling laws of this new architecture, demonstrating that it not only exhibits favorable scaling properties but outperforms traditional models.
elements.sent-2411-006: In our experiments, we train networks with up to 20 million memory slots.
elements.sent-2411-007: The results show that our method achieves state-of-the-art inference speed and model performance within a given computational budget.

elements.sent-2411-008: To address computational issues, the Mixture of Experts (MoE)\citep{fedus2022switch,jiang2024mixtral and Product Key Memory (PKM)\citep{lample2019large have been introduced.}}
elements.sent-2411-009: MoE selectively activates parameters, boosting training efficiency but impairing inference time due to increased memory access.
elements.sent-2411-010: PKM maintains consistent memory access with fewer value embeddings but its performance is significantly worse than MoE.
elements.sent-2411-011: This slowdown, as depicted in Figure \ref{fig:inference_time (c), stems from high memory access demands, highlighting its inefficiency in inference scenarios.}
elements.sent-2411-012: The primary challenge is how to match or even surpass the effectiveness of the MoE model while maintaining memory access levels comparable to those of dense models.
elements.sent-2411-013: In this paper, we introduce UltraMem, an architecture that builds upon and extends the concepts from PKM.
elements.sent-2411-014: UltraMem incorporates large-scale, ultra-sparse memory layers that significantly enhance computational efficiency and reduce inference latency while maintaining or even improving model performance across various benchmarks.
elements.sent-2411-015: This architecture not only supports the deployment of highly effective language models in resource-constrained environments but also opens up new avenues for constructing even larger models without the previously associated prohibitive costs.
elements.sent-2411-016: In summary, we make the following contributions:.
elements.sent-2411-017: UltraMem is greatly enhanced compared to PKM, and outperforms MoE at same scale.
elements.sent-2411-018: Compared to PKM, UltraMem truly possesses the prerequisites for training large-scale models on extensive computational resources and has undergone comprehensive experimental validation.
elements.sent-2411-019: UltraMem has significantly lower memory access cost during inference compared to MoE.
elements.sent-2411-020: Under common inference batch sizes, it can be up to 6 times faster than MoE with the same parameters and calculations.
elements.sent-2411-021: The inference speed of UltraMem is almost identical to that of a dense model with equivalent computational resources.
elements.sent-2411-022: We have verified the scaling ability of UltraMem.
elements.sent-2411-023: Similar to MoE, UltraMem has strong scaling ability, and we have observed stronger scaling ability than MoE.

elements.sent-2411-197: As memory table scales towards billions even trillions of parameters, model parallelism becomes essential to distribute model parameters and optimizer states across multiple devices to ensure they fit into device memory and are trainable within a reasonable time frame.
elements.sent-2411-198: We leverages Megatron's \citep{shoeybi2019megatron,narayanan2021efficient 3D parallelism (pipeline parallelism, data parallelism, and tensor parallelism) for training.}
elements.sent-2411-199: However, several parallelism modifications are required to support the memory table effectively.
elements.sent-2411-200: Because pipeline parallelism cannot address scenarios where a single layer's parameters exceed the memory capacity of a single device, and tensor parallelism is typically limited to a relatively small group of GPUs, making it insufficient to meet the memory table's memory requirements.
elements.sent-2411-201: Consequently, we propose sharding the memory table across a combination of data parallel and tensor parallel groups or its subgroups, to ensure efficient distribution and scalability.
elements.sent-2411-202: The memory table can be partitioned either number-wise or dimension-wise.
elements.sent-2411-203: The entire process of number-wise and dimension-wise partitioning, along with their communication volume analysis and guidance on how to choose the appropriate partitioning method, is detailed in Appendix \ref{appendix: partitioning .}
elements.sent-2411-204: In our structural improvements, halving v\_dim can simultaneously reduce the communication overhead for both number-wise and dimension-wise partitioning.
elements.sent-2411-205: However, increasing top-$m$ will proportionally increase the communication overhead.
elements.sent-2411-206: Additionally, Implicit Value Expansion, due to the increase in the size of values after weighted sum pooling, will further impact the communication volume for dimension-wise partitioning.
elements.sent-2411-207: To further augment performance, several key modifications have been implemented: Fused Lookup-Reduce Operator: This newly introduced operator accelerates computations and reduces memory usage by combining the lookup and weighted sum pooling operations into a single, more efficient step.
elements.sent-2411-208: Asynchronous Execution Strategy: Recognizing the benefits of cross-layer utilization of the memory layer, we have adopted an asynchronous execution strategy.
elements.sent-2411-209: This strategic choice allows for the concurrent processing of memory calculations alongside dense network operations, substantially enhancing the overall system performance.
elements.sent-2411-210: These enhancements demonstrate the efficacy of our parallelism strategy within the Megatron framework, paving the way for more efficient training of large-scale models.
elements.sent-2411-211: Figure \ref{fig:partitioning shows the process of number-wise and dimension-wise partition.}
elements.sent-2411-212: For number-wise partitioning, we first perform an all-to-all on indices to distribute them to the corresponding devices.
elements.sent-2411-213: After the lookup operation, the results are sent back to the original devices, then do weighted sum pooling.
elements.sent-2411-214: For dimension-wise partitioning, we need to perform an all-gather operation on indices to obtain all indices across devices.
elements.sent-2411-215: The lookup operation is then performed, dimension-wise partitioning allows the results to be sent back to each device after completing the weighted sum pooling.

The weighted sum pooling step is omitted in the diagram.} \label{fig:partitioning}
elements.sent-2411-216: Assuming the memory table is distributed across P processors, the communication volume can be described as follows: Number-wise Partitioning Communication Volume (not considering indices deduplication):.
elements.sent-2411-217: All-to-all transmission of indices: $sizeof(int) \times bs \times topm \times (P - 1) / P$.
elements.sent-2411-218: All-to-all transmission of embeddings after lookup: $sizeof(bfloat16) \times bs \times topm \times v\_dim \times (P - 1) / P$.
elements.sent-2411-222: All-to-all transmission of embeddings post-lookup reduction: $sizeof(bfloat16) \times bs \times v\_dim \times (P - 1) / P$.
elements.sent-2411-223: Here $v\_dim$ is the value dimension, $bs$ is the batch size times sequence length.
elements.sent-2411-224: Figure \ref{fig:communication shows the relationship between P and v\_dim for communication volume of these two partitioning methods, helping us choose the appropriate partitioning method under a fixed configuration.}

elements.figure-2411-019: \begin{center} \includegraphics[width=0.8\textwidth]{image_big/value_proj_big.pdf} \end{center} \caption{Flow of Implicit Value Expansion (IVE), here $E=4$, $m=16$. \textcolor{black}{IVE reduces memory access and scales up memory size by expanding the memory table virtually.
